title: Chrysanthemum leontopodium
taxonomy: Chrysanthemum leontopodium is classified in the kingdom Plantae, in the family Asteraceae, and in the genus Chrysanthemum. This plant is further divided into several species, including Chrysanthemum leontopodium var. alpinum and Chrysanthemum leontopodium var. parnassicum. The classification of Chrysanthemum leontopodium is a matter of ongoing research and some sources may group it differently. The plant's classification is often debated among botanists due to its complex relationships with other species in the genus Chrysanthemum. Further research is needed to fully understand the classification of Chrysanthemum leontopodium.
uses: Chrysanthemum leontopodium, commonly known as crow's daisy or edelweiss, has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for its purported health benefits. Its leaves and flowers are said to have anti-inflammatory properties, making it useful for treating conditions such as arthritis and other joint-related issues. The plant is also believed to have antimicrobial and antiviral properties, which can help to prevent infections and promote wound healing. In addition, Chrysanthemum leontopodium is thought to have antioxidant and immunomodulatory effects, which may help to protect against oxidative stress and support the immune system. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy of these uses and to establish safe dosages.

parts: Chrysanthemum leontopodium, also known as the Snow Lotus, is a highly valued plant in traditional Chinese medicine. preparations: Chrysanthemum leontopodium, also known as Edelweiss, has been used in traditional herbal medicine to treat various ailments. intro: Chrysanthemum leontopodium, also known as crown daisy or pyrethrum, is a perennial plant in the Asteraceae family, native to the mountainous regions of China. The plant has been traditionally used in Chinese medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, headache, and inflammation, due to its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic properties. The bioactive constituents of Chrysanthemum leontopodium include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with Chrysanthemum leontopodium include tea, tinctures, and infusions, which are used to treat respiratory and digestive issues, as well as to promote overall well-being.
